slides.title: As we step into the topic of convolution, let’s outline what we’ll be exploring
bullets: We’ll begin by revisiting shift-invariant linear systems, grounding ourselves in a concept we touched on earlier. Then, we’ll move into two important formulations of convolution: continuous convolution and discrete convolution.

Each of them plays a crucial role depending on the context — continuous for signals that evolve smoothly over time or space, and discrete for digital signals and images. Through this lecture, we’ll examine both perspectives and their practical implications.
By the end of the course, you’ll not only understand how convolution works — you'll also appreciate why it matters in system analysis, image reconstruction, and the broader field of medical imaging.

slides.title: Let’s refresh our picture of a linear, shift-invariant system using this ripple
bullets: Imagine dropping a pebble into a calm pond.
You get a circular ripple pattern that spreads outward.
Now drop the pebble somewhere else.
You still get the same kind of ripple pattern,
it just starts from a different location.
That idea, “same response, just shifted,” is what we call shift invariance.
The system behaves the same way no matter where the input happens.
If you shift the input in space, the output shifts in the same way,
but the shape of the response stays the same.
Now connect that to medical imaging.
In an ideal setting, if the patient has not changed,
and the scan conditions are comparable,
the imaging system should produce consistent results.
So repeating a scan should not create a completely different picture just because of position or timing.
That consistency is the intuition behind invariance assumptions.
Next is linearity, and linearity has two parts.
First is additivity.
If two separate effects are present,
the system’s response to both together should be the sum of the responses to each one separately.
Second is homogeneity, or scaling.
If the strength of the input doubles,
the strength of the output should also double,
at least within the range where the system is behaving linearly.
When both additivity and homogeneity hold,
we get the superposition principle.
And that is why linear, shift-invariant models are so useful:
they are predictable and analyzable,
and they lead directly to tools like convolution and Fourier methods.
So keep the ripple picture in mind.
It’s a simple visual that captures what we mean by shift invariance,
and why linear, shift-invariant systems are such a central model in imaging.

slides.title: To understand convolution deeply, let’s frame it through a familiar story from p
bullets: You might recall the idea — sometimes attributed to Newton — that the universe began with a single impulse, a sort of divine kick that set everything into motion. Whether metaphor or mechanics, this idea captures something fundamental.

In physics, we often model this idea of an initial force as an impulse — a very short burst of energy that sets a system into motion. For instance, think of hitting a golf ball with a club. That brief push is an impulse, and the ball's subsequent motion is the system’s response.
slides.fullText:
To understand convolution deeply, let’s frame it through a familiar story from physics.
You might recall the idea — sometimes attributed to Newton — that the universe began with a single impulse, a sort of divine kick that set everything into motion. Whether metaphor or mechanics, this idea captures something fundamental.
In physics, we often model this idea of an initial force as an impulse — a very short burst of energy that sets a system into motion. For instance, think of hitting a golf ball with a club. That brief push is an impulse, and the ball's subsequent motion is the system’s response.
slides.script: Let's explore the connection between convolution and this physical concept of an impulse. The idea of an initial force setting a system into motion is a powerful metaphor for understanding convolution. It helps us see convolution as a way to describe how a system responds to an impulse, whether that impulse is a brief push or a more complex input. This physical perspective can deepen our understanding of convolution and its applications.

slides.title: Let’s take a closer look at the idea of an impulse, using this golf-ball picture
bullets: When you hit a stationary golf ball, the force from the club acts only for a very short time.
It is a quick, concentrated push.
But even though it lasts only a moment, the effect is lasting.
That short burst of force changes the ball’s momentum, and the ball starts moving.
That is the basic intuition behind an impulse:
a brief input that produces a measurable change in the system.
Now bring this back to system analysis.
We often ask a very specific question:
if I hit a system with an idealized impulse, what does the system do afterward?
The output we observe after that impulse is called the impulse response.
And it is incredibly important, because for a linear, shift-invariant system,
the impulse response completely characterizes the system.
Once you know the impulse response, you can predict the output for any input signal,
not by guessing, but by using convolution.
So you can think of convolution as the tool that builds a complicated response
out of many shifted and scaled impulse responses.
So this simple golf example is a doorway to a big idea:
impulse response tells you how a system behaves,
and convolution tells you how the system responds to everything else.

slides.title: Let’s look at how impulse relates to momentum — a key concept in both physics an
bullets: Momentum, as you may recall, is simply mass multiplied by velocity.

So if an object is at rest, it has zero momentum. When you apply a force over a period of time — say, when you hit a tennis ball with a racket — the object gains velocity. That change in velocity reflects a change in momentum.
Now here’s the essential link:
Impulse is defined as force multiplied by the time over which it acts.
We start with Newton’s second law, which tells us:
Force equals mass times acceleration — or simply,
F equals m a.
Now, acceleration is defined as the change in velocity divided by the change in time — so we write:
a equals delta v over delta t.
If we substitute that into Newton’s law, we get:
F times delta t equals m times delta v.
This equation shows us something powerful:
Impulse — that’s force times time — equals the change in momentum.
tells us that impulse causes the change in momentum.
It’s the way to model how mechanical systems respond to external inputs, and the idea applies to other input-output relationships as well.

slides.title: Building on what we just discussed, this slide is showing something very elegant
bullets: Look at the different shapes on the plot.
Some are tall and very narrow.
Some are shorter but much wider.
Even though the shapes look different, the key message is this:
for impulse, the exact shape is not the main thing.
What really matters is the area under the curve.
That area is the total “overall effect” of the force applied over time.
So a small force applied for a longer time can produce the same impulse
as a large force applied for a very short time,
as long as the areas match.
Now translate that idea into system thinking.
Often, what drives the system’s response is not a single instantaneous value,
but the accumulated influence of the input over time.
That is exactly the mindset behind convolution.
Convolution is basically a structured way of adding up contributions across time:
you take a response shape, you slide it, scale it, and sum up the overlap.
So this impulse picture is a nice bridge from physical intuition
to the mathematical tool we are about to use.

slides.title: Now let’s extend that idea.
bullets: Impulse isn’t limited to rectangular shapes. We can see in this graph that different Gaussian curves — whether wide or narrow, flat or sharp — can all serve as impulse-like inputs.

What matters is the area under each curve. This area represents the total impulse delivered by the force, regardless of how the function looks. As long as that area remains the same, the impact on the system is the same.
This insight is critical in both physics and engineering: we often abstract away the exact shape of a signal and focus instead on its integrated effect — the impulse.

slides.title: Now we come to an important mathematical idea: the limiting process.
bullets: This is how we build an ideal impulse in math.
We start with a simple pulse-shaped function, which the slide calls d sub tau of t.
Here is the definition.
d sub tau of t equals one over two tau when t is between minus tau and plus tau,
and it equals zero everywhere else.
So it is just a flat rectangle centered at zero.
The width is two tau,
and the height is one over two tau.
Now notice what that choice does.
If you multiply the height by the width,
you get one over two tau times two tau, which is exactly one.
So no matter what tau is,
the area under the curve stays equal to one.
Now imagine making tau smaller and smaller.
The rectangle gets narrower,
and at the same time it gets taller,
but the total area stays fixed at one.
That is the key idea behind the Dirac delta.
In the limit as tau goes to zero,
the pulse collapses into something infinitely narrow and infinitely tall,
but with a finite total area of one.
The slide summarizes this in two statements.
First, the pointwise value d sub tau of t goes to zero as tau goes to zero,
because the pulse becomes narrower and narrower.
Second, the total area, which we can call I of tau,
stays equal to one in the limit.
So the delta function is not a normal function in the usual sense.
It is an idealized impulse:
zero everywhere except concentrated at a single instant,
yet still carrying a total “weight” of one.
And this is exactly what we need for system analysis.
Once we define an impulse cleanly,
we can define the impulse response,
and then convolution will tell us how the system responds to any input.

slides.title: Let’s pause for a second and ask the question this slide is asking:
bullets: if we already understand the effect of an impulse,
why do we need a limiting process at all?
The reason is that the limiting process gives us an ideal impulse that is thorough and “perfect,”
in the same way that the limit definition gives us a perfect definition of a derivative.
In real life, every impulse has some finite width and some shape.
But in system theory, we want a universal model that captures only what matters,
without being tied to a particular physical waveform.
Now that leads to the next question on the slide:
what is the shape of the delta function?
And the honest answer is:
we don’t really know, and in most cases, we don’t need to care.
Why?
Because for the delta function, the important feature is not the detailed shape,
it is the overall effect, which is captured by its total area, or total weight.
Different narrow pulses can look different,
but if they are all “impulse-like” and have the same total area,
they represent the same idealized input in the limit.
So you can think of it this way:
as long as the impulse delivers the same total amount in an instant,
the system theory treats it as the same delta input.
And that’s why the slide says “all shapes are possible.”
If you refuse to specify the shape, then many shapes could fit,
and you only know the exact shape if you measure a real waveform.
The “quantum mechanics” line is just a playful analogy:
without measurement, you can imagine many possibilities,
but in our math model, we intentionally avoid the details,
because the delta function is meant to represent only the ideal overall effect.

slides.title: Let’s take a deeper look at the Dirac delta function — one of the most powerful
bullets: The delta function is what we call a generalized function, or in mathematical terms, a distribution. It helps us model a perfect impulse — something that delivers a concentrated effect at a single moment in time.
Now, here’s how we describe it mathematically:
We say that delta of t minus t naught equals zero for all values of t not equal to t naught.
And, when we integrate this delta function from minus infinity to infinity, the result is one.
What does that mean?

It means the function is zero everywhere — except at one specific point in time, which we call t naught. At that single point, it has an infinitely sharp spike, but the area under the spike is exactly one. That area is what we call the impulse.
So even though we can’t see or draw the spike perfectly, we know that its total effect — the impulse — is one. That’s what matters. The effect is important, but the exact shape doesn’t matter.
Now, let’s shift to the discrete version of the delta function — something we use in digital systems.
We define delta of n like this:
It equals one when n is zero,
and it equals zero for all other values of n.
In other words, this function is zero everywhere — except at n equals zero, where it has a value of one.
It’s a single pulse — like a quick tap — at a specific point in time.
We can also shift this pulse. For example, delta of n minus two just moves the pulse to position n equals two.
This simple pulse is incredibly useful. It forms the building block for discrete-time convolution, which we’ll talk about next.
So both the continuous and discrete versions of the delta function help us analyze how systems respond to input — whether we’re dealing with real-world signals or digital data.

slides.title: Before we use the delta function to talk about sampling, let’s recall one key id
bullets: the mean value theorem for integrals.
Look at the picture.
We have a continuous function y equals f of x, drawn over the interval from a to b.
The shaded region represents the area under the curve, which is the integral of f from a to b.
Now here is the statement of the theorem in plain language.
It says that somewhere between a and b,
there exists at least one point c,
so that the function value at that point, f of c,
is equal to the average height of the function over the whole interval.
And visually, that means this:
if you draw a horizontal line at y equals f of c,
you get a rectangle with base length b minus a,
and that rectangle has exactly the same area as the area under the curve.
So the idea is:
the total area can be represented as “average height times width,”
and the theorem guarantees that this average height actually occurs at some point c inside the interval.
We are going to use this intuition in the next step.
When the interval becomes very small,
the average value over that tiny interval starts to look like the value at a specific point.
And that is exactly the kind of thinking that leads into how the delta function acts like an ideal sampler.

Before we use the delta function to talk about sampling, let’s recall one key idea from calculus:
the mean value theorem for integrals.

Look at the picture.
We have a continuous function y equals f of x, drawn over the interval from a to b.
The shaded region represents the area under the curve, which is the integral of f from a to b.

Now here is the statement of the theorem in plain language.

It says that somewhere between a and b,
there exists at least one point c,
so that the function value at that point, f of c,
is equal to the average height of the function over the whole interval.

And visually, that means this:
if you draw a horizontal line at y equals f of c,
you get a rectangle with base length b minus a,
and that rectangle has exactly the same area as the area under the curve.

So the idea is:
the total area can be represented as “average height times width,”
and the theorem guarantees that this average height actually occurs at some point c inside the interval.

We are going to use this intuition in the next step.
When the interval becomes very small,
the average value over that tiny interval starts to look like the value at a specific point.
And that is exactly the kind of thinking that leads into how the delta function acts like an ideal sampler.
slides.script: The mean value theorem tells us that there's a point where the function's value matches the average value over the entire interval. This means that if we zoom in enough, we'll find a point where the function's value equals the average height of the curve over that interval. This is a fundamental idea in calculus that will be useful later in understanding sampling and reconstruction in medical imaging.

slides.title: Let’s now connect this idea directly to the delta function and its role in sampl
bullets: Imagine you have a continuous function — let’s call it f of t. Now, if you multiply this function by a shifted delta function, specifically delta of t minus t naught, and then integrate, something powerful happens:
You end up extracting the value of f at t naught. In other words, the delta function acts like a perfect sampling tool.
Mathematically, this is shown through a limiting process. As the width of the delta function shrinks toward zero, its height increases — but the total area remains one. That’s key.

Inside this narrow window around t naught, the function f is essentially flat — and using the mean value theorem, we know there must be some point where the function's value equals the average over that interval. As the window becomes infinitesimally small, that point converges exactly to t naught.
So what do we get?
The integral from minus infinity to infinity of delta of t minus t naught, times f of t, d t — equals f of t naught.
This is called the sampling property of the delta function. It’s as if the delta function "reaches into" the function and pulls out the value at a single point.

Even more fascinating — we can actually rebuild any continuous function using this idea. By stacking many delta functions across time, each weighted by the function’s value at that point, we can reconstruct the entire function. Think of it like slicing the function into tiny pieces — each slice tells us something, and when you add them all together, you get the full picture again.

This is a fundamental concept in digital signal processing and is crucial for understanding how to represent and manipulate signals in the digital domain.

slides.title: Now let’s translate this delta idea into the discrete world.
bullets: In the discrete case, instead of a continuous curve, we deal with samples on a grid.
One helpful way to visualize a discrete signal is as a collection of little rectangular bars,
one bar at each integer location.
That is exactly the picture you see on this slide.
At the top, the slide is reminding you that we can build a function out of shifted building blocks.
One common building block is the rectangular “gate” function, written as rect of t, or Pi of t.
And its definition is simple:
it is one inside a small interval around zero,
it is zero outside,
and it takes the value one-half right at the endpoints.
So it is basically a unit-width pulse centered at zero.
Now move to the bottom, where the main point is.
The discrete delta function, delta of n, is defined like this:
it equals one when n equals zero,
and it equals zero everywhere else.
So it is a single spike at n equals zero.
And if we shift it, for example delta of n minus two,
the spike moves to n equals two.
Everything else stays zero.
Why is this so useful?
Because these shifted impulses are like the “basis elements” for discrete signals.
Any discrete sequence can be written as a sum of scaled and shifted delta functions:
each delta picks out one sample location,
and the scaling coefficient sets the value at that location.
So you can think of it like building a picture out of pixels.
Each pixel is one location,
and delta functions let us represent the whole discrete signal as a clean sum of those locations.

slides.title: Here’s the idea visually.
bullets: The gray curve is the original continuous-time function, f of t.
And the red staircase is a piecewise-constant approximation of that curve.
So what we are doing is this:
we divide the time axis into small intervals,

That constant is chosen to match the function value on that interval,
so each step is like a little rectangular “gate.”
Now, the key point of the slide title is that this staircase signal can be written
as a sum of shifted rect, or gate, functions.
Each step is one gate function located over a particular time bin,
and multiplied by the appropriate height.
When you add all those scaled gates together, you get the full red staircase.
So this is one way to think about sampling and approximation:
a continuous signal can be represented, approximately, by a sum of simple building blocks,
and here the building block is a rectangular pulse.
And later, when we shrink the width of those rectangles,
this picture also connects naturally to the impulse viewpoint,
where signals are represented as sums of shifted, scaled impulses.

slides.title: Now let’s take the next step.
bullets: This slide is showing that we can represent a sampled signal as a sum of delta functions.
Look at the gray curve as the underlying continuous function.
Then at each sampling time, we take one sample value.
The red arrows show those sampled values.
Mathematically, we can think of each sample as a scaled delta located at that time.
So at time t0 we place a delta scaled by f of t0,
at time t1 we place a delta scaled by f of t1,
and so on.
When you add up all of those shifted, scaled deltas,
you get a compact representation of the sampled signal.
So the key idea is:
delta functions act like “selectors.”
Each delta isolates one location in time,
and its scaling coefficient carries the sample value at that location.
That’s why we say any discrete-time signal can be written as a sum of shifted deltas.
And this representation becomes extremely useful once we start talking about convolution,
because convolution with deltas is very easy to analyze.

bullets: Here we observe a powerful mathematical concept: any discrete function — let’s call it g of n — can be expressed as a weighted sum of shifted delta functions. That is, we take the delta function, shift it to the appropriate position, and scale it by the function’s value at that point. When we add all these together, we recover the original function.
Here you can see the mathematical expression in this slide.
This formula tells us that each sample of g contributes one impulse, located at n equals k, and scaled by g at that k. When we add up all these scaled and shifted impulses, we rebuild the function g.
Let’s look at a specific example to make this concrete.
Suppose your discrete function consists of just a few nonzero points. The first point is at n equals 0 with value 1 — that’s just delta of n.
The second point is at n equals 1 with a value of negative 1 — so we write that as minus delta of n minus 1.
And the third point is at n equals 2 with a value of 1.5 — which becomes 1.5 times delta of n minus 2.
By adding these three impulses together, we effectively reconstruct the original signal, point by point.

This approach is not only elegant, it’s also foundational in signal processing and linear systems. It allows us to treat any discrete-time signal as a sum of building blocks — scaled and shifted impulses — making analysis and filtering much easier later on.
We’ll continue building on this idea when we introduce convolution, which is just the response of a system to this kind of delta-based representation.

slides.title: So far, we’ve looked at delta functions in two settings:
bullets: the continuous-time version and the discrete-time version.
And the big idea is that both give us a way to build signals out of simple building blocks.
In the continuous case, we think in terms of integrals and the Dirac delta.
In the discrete case, we think in terms of summations and the discrete impulse.
Now, this slide is reminding us of something important:
the boundary between “continuous” and “discrete” is not always obvious in the real world.
Look at the pictures at the top.
On the left, you see a staircase-style picture, which is discrete:
it is made of individual steps.
On the right, you see a smooth curve, which is continuous.
And the fence photo at the bottom gives the same intuition in a physical way.
From far away, a fence can look like one continuous boundary.
But when you get closer, you realize it is made of individual boards,
so it is actually discrete.
That’s exactly how many real systems behave.
At one scale, a continuous model is the most convenient and accurate.
At another scale, a discrete model makes more sense.
So in engineering, we choose the viewpoint that helps us solve the problem.
Summation is the natural language for discrete signals.
Integration is the natural language for continuous signals.
And conceptually, they are closely related.
With that foundation in place, we’re ready for the next step:
we will connect delta functions directly to shift-invariant systems,
and that will lead us naturally into convolution, which is a key tool in imaging and signal processing.

slides.title: Let’s take a look at this graph.
bullets: This impulse response, shown here as h of n, is our key piece of knowledge.
I’m not asking you to start from nothing.
I’m giving you the system’s reaction to a standardized input — the delta function applied at time zero.
Once you have that, you’re ready.
Because if I give the system a more general input — not just a single impulse, but any arbitrary shape —
you can still figure out the output.
So the question becomes:
How do you compute the output from this known impulse response and a general input?
That’s exactly where convolution comes in.
Convolution is the operation that combines the input signal with the impulse response,
to produce the final output.
It’s absolutely essential in imaging and signal processing.

slides.title: In a shift-invariant system, if you know the system’s response to an impulse at
bullets: That’s because shift-invariance means the system behaves the same way, no matter where or when you apply the impulse.
If you apply an impulse at a different time or position, you can infer the output by simply shifting the known response accordingly.
And because the system is linear, you have additivity and homogeneity.
If you scale an input, the output will be scaled in the same way.
If you add multiple inputs, the output will be the sum of individual outputs due to each input.
This is exactly why knowing the impulse response of a system tells you everything about how that system behaves — no matter what input you give it.
To summarize, for a shift-invariant linear system, given any input, we want to compute the output.
The critical information we rely on is the system’s impulse response.
If we know how the system reacts to a delta function at time zero,
we can use that as a building block to find the output for any input.

slides.title: To be specific, now let’s see how we actually compute the output y of n of a shi
bullets: Suppose I give you a system characterized by its impulse response, written as h of n, which is shown on the 2nd line.
That means: if you feed the system a discrete delta function as input, the output will be h of n.
This response — h of n — is the system’s character.
But what if you give the system a more general input, say x of n?
What will the output y of n be, which is what I just formulated on the 1st line, and a very important question.
Let’s break it down step by step.
Since the system is shift-invariant, if you shift the input by k, the output also shifts by k, shown on the 3rd line.
So if the input is delta of n minus k, the output becomes h of n minus k.
Then, because the system is linear, if we scale the input by x of k,
Consequently, the output is also scaled by x of k.
That gives us x of k times h of n minus k on the 4th line.
Now, to get the total output, we add up all of these scaled and shifted responses.
Mathematically, this gives us the convolution sum, on the right-hand side of the last two lines:
y of n equals the sum over k from minus infinity to infinity of x of k times h of n minus k.
This is how the convolution is defined, and also why we need this concept.
It shows why the impulse response is so powerful — it’s all you need to compute the system’s output for any input.
Now, convolution might seem a bit tricky at first, especially because of the flipping and shifting involved —
that negative k inside h of n minus k could appear confusing.
But if you follow the logic step-by-step, the structure becomes clear.
This convolution concept is fundamental in linear systems and engineering —
and that’s why we dedicate so much attention to understanding convolution.

slides.title: Now let’s apply the same idea in the continuous case.
bullets: Again, you’re given the system’s response to a delta function, which we call h of t.
If we shift the delta to t minus tau, the output becomes h of t minus tau.
In other words, shifting the input shifts the output in the same way — that’s shift-invariance.
Next, we scale the input by x of tau.
Because the system is linear, the output also gets scaled — giving us x of tau times h of t minus tau.
Finally, to build the full output for a general input signal,
we integrate over all time — summing these scaled and shifted responses.
This gives us the continuous-time convolution integral:
You can think of this as collecting all the weighted impulse responses across time,
and combining them to form the system’s output, where we have utilized the sampling property of the delta function.
This is how linear, shift-invariant systems behave in continuous time —
and this operation, convolution, lies at the heart of modeling and understanding them.
Finally, we have assumed that all involved integrals or summations on the previous slide converge, so mathematically well defined.

But I know from experience that convolution can feel confusing at first.That’s why it really helps to look at a concrete example.
Let’s move on to one now.
slides.script: So whether we're dealing with discrete or continuous systems, Convolution is the tool we use to compute the output. In both cases, the idea is the same: express the input as a collection of impulses, use the impulse response to compute the output for each impulse, and then sum or integrate all those individual responses to get the final output. This process is captured using these two key equations, which show the discrete and continuous mathematical formulations of convolution.

spotlightEvents.label: Convolution in discrete and continuous systems
Same idea for both discrete and continuous systems
Express input as a collection of impulses

slides.title: Let’s do a hands-on example of discrete convolution, using the “two hands” pictu
bullets: We have two discrete sequences.
Think of the red one as x of n, the input.
Think of the green one as h of n, the impulse response.
Now here is the key step that always confuses people at first:
in convolution we do not use h of k.
We use h of n minus k.
That “minus k” means we have to flip h before we slide it.
So use your hands to remember it.
Your left hand is x of n.
Your right hand is h of n.
To get h of n minus k, you first flip your right hand,
like turning it around so you see the back side.
That flip is the time-reversal step.
After flipping, we shift it.
And we shift it across x step by step.
Now we compute the output one index at a time.
At n equals zero,
we place the flipped h so its zero point lines up at n equals zero.
Then we multiply whatever samples overlap,
and we add up those products.
That sum is y of zero.
At n equals one,
we shift the flipped h one step to the right.
Again, multiply the overlapping samples and add them up.
That gives y of one.
And we keep doing the same routine:
shift, multiply, sum.
As we slide through,
the overlap between the two sequences first grows,
then reaches a maximum when the two shapes overlap the most,
and then shrinks again as they slide past each other.
That is why the convolution output rises to a peak and then falls back down.
So this is the core algorithm of discrete convolution:
flip one sequence,
shift it,
multiply point-by-point,
and sum the overlap.
If you can visualize that process, the formula stops feeling mysterious.

slides.title: Now let’s put actual numbers into our hand example and see the result.
bullets: On the left hand, think of the five samples as
five, four, three, two, one.
That’s our input sequence x.
On the right hand, the samples go the other way:
one, two, three, four, five.
That’s our impulse response h.
Now we do exactly what convolution tells us to do:
we flip the right-hand sequence,
we slide it across the left-hand sequence,
and at each shift we multiply the overlapping entries and add them up.
Each one of those sums becomes one output sample.
And that is exactly what the MATLAB code on the slide is doing.
We define:
x equals [5 4 3 2 1]
h equals [1 2 3 4 5]
Then we compute:
y equals conv(x, h)
And finally we plot y.
If you look at the plot, you see a clear peak in the middle.
That makes sense, because the middle position is where the overlap between the two sequences is the strongest,
so the sum of products is the largest.
Then as we slide past that point, the overlap decreases,
so the output falls back down.
One quick bookkeeping rule to remember:
if x has length N1 and h has length N2,
then the convolution result has length N1 plus N2 minus one.
In this example, both are length five, so the output length is nine,
which matches the plot.
So this is the big takeaway:
convolution is not mysterious.
It is flip, shift, multiply, and sum.
And for linear, shift-invariant systems, that operation is the core tool that tells you how the system responds to any input.

slides.title: Let’s look at another example, this time directly from medical imaging.
bullets: This picture is about a gamma camera imaging a hypothetical kidney.
So instead of talking about iodine in X-ray, let’s stay with what the slide is showing:
a tracer signal being detected by a gamma camera over time.
On the left, you see an “impulse input.”
You can think of that as an idealized, very short injection of a radioactive tracer,
almost like a delta function in time.
Now look at the diagram in the middle.
The tracer does not travel through a single clean path.
It moves through multiple routes, and each route has a different transit time.
That’s why the branches are labeled with different delays, like 3 minutes, 4 minutes, 5 minutes, 6 minutes.
So what does the gamma camera measure?
On the right, you see the impulse response:
a sequence of values, H1, H2, H3, and so on, appearing at different times.
Each piece of that response corresponds to tracer activity arriving through a different pathway,
with a different delay and a different weight.
Now here is the key systems idea.
If the input were a perfect impulse, then the measured output would be the impulse response itself.
But in reality, injections are not perfect impulses.
The input is spread out in time.
And when a spread-out input passes through a linear, time-invariant system,
the output is the convolution of the input with the impulse response.
So this is a very concrete example of why convolution matters in medical imaging.
It gives us a clean way to describe how a time-varying tracer input is transformed
by the body’s transport dynamics and then observed by an imaging device like a gamma camera.

slides.title: In reality, the contrast isn’t delivered as a perfect impulse —
bullets: it’s injected over a period of time, with more at the start and less later, forming a time curve.
We can think of this non-ideal input as a sum of small impulses occurring at different times —
each one representing a fraction of the total injected dose.
For each of these small impulses, we already know the system’s response —
that’s the impulse response we’ve discussed.
So what do we do?
We scale each impulse response based on how much contrast was injected at that moment,
shift it in time according to when it was injected,
and then add all the individual responses together.
What we get is the system’s total response — the final signal that’s measured by the detector.
This is a perfect visualization of discrete convolution in a real-world setting.
It shows how imaging systems handle non-ideal, time-varying inputs using the exact same principles we’ve learned.
It may take some time for these ideas to fully sink in,
but they form the foundation for understanding how imaging systems process signals and generate useful images.

slides.title: Now, let’s work through a more abstract — and slightly more mathematical — examp
bullets: I like to call this a minds-on example.
This example is based on continuous convolution, and while it’s not difficult, it does require you to think like in calculus.
So let’s take our time with it.
With convolution, the basic process is this:
We flip one function, shift it, multiply them point by point, and then integrate over the region where they overlap.
At first, that might sound a little mechanical. So let’s break it down clearly, step by step.
We’re working with two functions here:
f of t, shown in red. This is a triangular function — it starts at zero, rises to a peak at t equals 2, and then drops back to zero.
g of t, shown in purple. This is a rectangular pulse that spans from t equals minus 2 to plus 2, with a constant value of 3.
Now, because g of t is symmetric, flipping it across the vertical axis doesn’t change its shape —
which actually makes our job easier.
So we’ll flip g to get g of t minus tau, and then slide it across f of tau,
evaluating the convolution at each position t.
Here’s the key part:
Both functions are compactly supported, meaning they’re only non-zero over a specific range.
That means the overlap only happens during certain intervals.
So to compute the convolution, we’ll handle it case by case — based on the value of t.

slides.script: Let's dive into this example step by step, breaking down the process of continuous convolution. We'll start with two functions, f of t, shown in red, and g of t, shown in blue, and apply the basic rules of convolution to understand the output.

spotlightEvents.label: Two Functions f of t and g of t

slides.title: 👉 Case 1: When t is less than –2
bullets: At this stage, the flipped and shifted version of g, written as g of t minus tau,
is positioned so far to the left that it does not overlap with f of tau at all.
That means the product of the two functions is zero everywhere,
so the convolution result y of t is also zero.
This is our first insight:
No overlap means no contribution to the integral, and the output is zero.
Case 2: When t is between –2 and 0
Now we begin to see partial overlap.
As g of t minus tau slides to the right, part of it starts to enter the region where f of tau is non-zero.
Let’s break this down mathematically:
Over the region where they overlap, f of tau is defined as
f of tau equals negative tau plus 2, valid from tau equals 0 to tau equals 2.
And the flipped rectangle, g of t minus tau, still has a constant height of 3 wherever it overlaps.
So we’re integrating the product of these two functions across the overlapping interval.
The simplifies convolution becomes:
3 times the integral of (–tau + 2), from 0 to 2 plus t.
If you evaluate this integral, you get:
y of t equals negative three-halves t squared plus 6.
So the convolution result in this case is a parabolic curve, rising as t approaches zero.
This illustrates an important idea:
Convolution is constructed from slices of overlapping areas —
and in each case, we’re summing the product over those intervals of overlap.

slides.script:
As t becomes more positive, the overlap between f of tau and g of t minus tau increases, and we start to see a contribution to the integral.
This is where the convolution result y of t begins to rise as t approaches zero.
The output starts to increase as the two functions start to overlap.

slides.title: Case 3: When t is between 0 and 2
bullets: In this case, the flipped and shifted version of g, that is, g of t minus tau,
completely overlaps the triangular function f of tau.
This means we integrate across the entire support of f of tau,
from tau equals 0 to 2.
Within this interval:
f of tau equals negative tau plus 2
g of t minus tau is constant at 3
So the convolution becomes:
y of t equals the integral from 0 to 2 of 3 times (–tau + 2), d tau
When we evaluate this, we get:
y of t equals 6
So, for t between 0 and 2, the convolution gives a flat region with a constant value of 6.
This happens because the area under the product is the same across the entire interval.
Case 4: When t is between 2 and 4
Now, f of tau begins to slide out of the rectangular window of g of t minus tau.
We’re back to a partial overlap, but this time on the opposite side —
the right edge of g is moving past f.
The new overlapping interval runs from
tau equals t minus 2 to tau equals 2
Again, we use:
f of tau equals negative tau plus 2
g of t minus tau equals 3
So the convolution becomes:
y of t equals the integral from t minus 2 to 2 of 3 times (–tau + 2), d tau
Evaluating this gives:
y of t equals three-halves t squared minus twelve t plus 24
This result traces out the descending part of the convolution curve,
as the overlap shrinks toward zero.
Case 5: When t is greater than or equal to 4
At this point, g of t minus tau has completely moved past f of tau.
There is no overlap between the two functions,
so the convolution result once again becomes:
y of t equals 0

slides.title: Alright, now we’re ready to put everything together.
bullets: Up to this point, we handled the convolution one interval at a time,
because the overlap between f of t and g of t changes as we slide one across the other.
And that overlap pattern is really the whole story.
No overlap means the convolution is zero.
Partial overlap gives us a changing value.
Full overlap gives us the maximum, and sometimes a flat region.
So when we combine all those cases, we get the complete piecewise result shown on this slide.
Here is the final answer.
For t less than minus two, there is no overlap, so y of t is zero.
For t between minus two and zero, the overlap is growing,
and the convolution becomes:
negative three over two times t squared plus six.
For t between zero and two, we have full overlap in the middle,
so the convolution stays constant at six.
For t between two and four, the overlap is shrinking,
and the convolution becomes:
three over two times t squared minus twelve t plus twenty four.
And for t greater than or equal to four, the overlap is gone again,
so y of t goes back to zero.
If you look at the plot at the bottom, you can see exactly what that means visually.
The curve rises as the overlap builds,
it levels off in the middle,
and then it falls as the overlap disappears.
That “bump” shape is exactly what we expect from convolution in continuous time.
So this slide is a nice checkpoint.
It shows that continuous convolution is not mysterious.
It is always the same workflow:
flip one function,
shift it,
multiply the overlap,
and integrate.
Then you repeat that logic over the different overlap intervals,
and finally you stitch the pieces together into one complete output.

slides.title: Now let’s tie all of this together with a very practical example: an RC circuit.
bullets: Even if you do not love circuits, you can treat this as a black box.
There is an input signal, x of t, and there is an output signal, y of t.
The box happens to be made of a resistor R and a capacitor C,
and we are looking at the voltage across the capacitor as the output.
Here is the key systems idea.
If this circuit is linear and time-invariant, then it is completely characterized by its impulse response, h of t.
So imagine we hit the input with an ideal impulse.
The output we observe is h of t.
For an RC circuit, that impulse response has a very simple shape:
it is a decaying exponential that starts at time zero and then fades away,
and the time scale is controlled by the product R times C.
Now look at the plots on the right side of the slide.
They are showing exactly what convolution means in practice.
On the left of each row you see an input x of t,
for example a short rectangular pulse, a longer pulse, or an impulse-like spike.
On the right of each row you see the output y of t,
along with the impulse response h of t for reference.
And the message is this:
different inputs produce different outputs,
but they all follow the same rule.
The output is the convolution of the input with the impulse response.
So the circuit is acting like a smoothing system.
A sharp input does not stay sharp.
It gets “spread out” in time according to that exponential response.
That is exactly what convolution captures.
And this is why we care beyond circuits.
The same mathematics shows up everywhere:
in signal filtering, in physiological time responses, and later in imaging systems.
Once you know the impulse response, convolution tells you how the system reacts to any input.

That is exactly what convolution captures.

And this is why we care beyond circuits.
The same mathematics shows up everywhere:
in signal filtering, in physiological time responses, and later in imaging systems.
Once you know the impulse response, convolution tells you how the system reacts to any input.
slides.script: Now let's tie all of this together with a very practical example: an RC circuit. Even if you don't love circuits, you can treat this as a black box with an input signal, x of t, and an output signal, y of t. The box consists of a resistor R and a capacitor C, and we're looking at the voltage across the capacitor as the output. If the circuit is linear and time-invariant, it's completely characterized by its impulse response, h of t.

slides.title: Now that you understand how 1D convolution works,
bullets: it’s actually quite easy to extend the idea to two dimensions, especially when working with images.
In 2D convolution, you’re essentially applying the same process —
but now, instead of just one variable like t, you’re working with two:
x and y in the discrete case, or tau one and tau two in the continuous case.
Let’s take a look at the discrete form first.
We can see the formula at the top of the slide:
f of x, y convolved with g of x, y equals the double sum
over n 1 and n 2 of f at n 1, n 2 times g at x minus n 1, y minus n 1.
Just like in 1D, we flip the kernel, shift it, multiply, and sum —
but now we do this across both rows and columns.
In the continuous case, we do the same thing using integrals.
We integrate over both variables — tau one and tau two —
to compute the total area under the product.
So the core principle stays the same:
Flip, shift, multiply, and sum or integrate —
but now it happens in two directions at once, across a 2D grid or surface.
This extension is especially important in image processing and medical imaging,
where we often apply filters, masks, or system responses across 2D signals like slices or projection data.
Whether you’re summing or integrating,
convolution gives you a systematic way to model how a signal transforms when passed through a system.

slides.title: In imaging, 2D convolution is a powerful tool for modeling blurring.
bullets: Let’s take a look at how this works using a simple example.
On the left, we see an input image represented as a matrix of pixel intensities.
Each number corresponds to the brightness level of a pixel.
In the middle, we see a 3 by 3 averaging mask.
All entries are 1, and we divide the result by 9 —
which means we’re averaging the values in a 3 by 3 window.
This operation is a 2D convolution:
we slide the mask across the image, and at each position,
we multiply corresponding elements, sum them up, and store the result in the center pixel of the output.
Let’s focus on the region that’s highlighted.
For the top-left example, the 3 by 3 neighborhood includes values like 1, 2, 3, and so on.
When we sum those values and divide by 9, we get the blurred output value, shown in yellow.
The same thing happens throughout the image.
Each pixel in the output image becomes the average of itself and its eight neighbors.
The result?
Sharp transitions are smoothed out, fine details are softened —
and the image takes on a blurred appearance.
This is a fundamental application of 2D convolution —
and it’s used not only for visual effects, but also as a preprocessing step in many medical imaging tasks,
like noise reduction and feature extraction.

slides.title: Let’s take a look at how blurring arises naturally in imaging systems — not just
bullets: Here we observe a key concept in optics called the point spread function, or PSF.
In theory, if you image a perfect point of light, it should appear as a sharp dot on the detector.
But in reality, that point gets blurred into a small disk — often called an Airy disk —
because of how light diffracts through the lens system.
This is the system’s impulse response in two dimensions.
So even if your input is just a single point — like a star in the night sky —
the output captured by your imaging system is a small, spread-out shape.
And when two bright points are placed too close together,
their point spread functions begin to overlap.
This overlap makes it difficult — or even impossible — to tell them apart in the final image.
This phenomenon is at the core of what we call the resolution limit in optical systems.
In fact, PSFs are not just a curiosity —
they’re mathematically modeled and measured,
and they define how sharp or blurry every part of an image will be.
So in medical imaging, astronomy, microscopy —
understanding the point spread function is essential to both image formation and image reconstruction.

slides.title: Now let’s talk about the reverse process of blurring — a technique called deconv
bullets: When an image is blurred, it’s often because the original scene has been convolved with a point spread function — or PSF.
We saw earlier that even a perfect point source becomes a blurred spot due to system limitations.
Deconvolution is the process of trying to undo that effect —
to recover the original image from its blurred version.
You can think of convolution like a kind of advanced multiplication:
we blend the signal with the system’s response.
In that same spirit, deconvolution behaves like an advanced form of division —
we’re trying to undo the blending, and isolate the original input.
We can see that clearly in the example on this slide.
The left image is a blurred photo of a car — the license plate is unreadable.
After deconvolution, the right image restores the clarity — now we can read the plate.
This kind of technique is used not just in photography, but in microscopy, astronomy, and especially in medical imaging,
where resolving fine detail can be critical.
It’s important to note that deconvolution is not always perfect —
in real-world systems, noise and distortion make it a challenging problem.
But with good modeling of the PSF and proper regularization,
we can often greatly enhance image quality.

slides.script: Deconvolution is the process of reversing the blurring effect to recover the original image from its blurred version. It's the opposite of convolution, where we try to undo the spreading out of the image caused by the system's limitations. Deconvolution is a clever technique that can restore sharpness to a blurred image, but it requires smart algorithms and good models.

slides.title: Here’s the idea in action.
bullets: This slide shows how inverse filtering works in the frequency domain.
A sharp image, when convolved with a point spread function, results in a blurred image.
In the Fourier domain, convolution becomes multiplication.
So, the Fourier transform of the blurred image equals
the product of the transforms of the original image and the PSF.
To reverse this, we perform division in the frequency domain —
this is the essence of inverse filtering.
In the red box below, the image on the right is the transform of the blurred image,
the center is the transform of the PSF,
and the left is the result of dividing them — recovering the original image spectrum.
Applying the inverse Fourier transform brings us back to the spatial domain.
This process forms the basis of many image restoration techniques.

slides.title: If this still feels a little abstract right now, that’s completely normal.
bullets: The picture will become much clearer once we introduce Fourier analysis.
For the moment, I want you to keep one simple analogy in mind.
In basic arithmetic, multiplication combines things, and division tries to undo that combination.
In signal and image processing, convolution plays the role of a more advanced kind of multiplication:
it combines an input with a system response, like blur or smoothing.
And deconvolution plays the role of the corresponding division:
given the blurred output and some knowledge of the system response,
we try to work backward and recover the original signal or image.
So just like integration can be thought of as “continuous addition,”
convolution can be thought of as “structured multiplication,”
and deconvolution is the hard inverse operation that tries to undo it.
We will return to this later, and Fourier methods will show you exactly why this analogy works so well.

If this still feels a little abstract right now, that’s completely normal.

slides.title: Returning to one-dimensional signals, convolution behaves like multiplication in
bullets: Commutative: h convolved with f equals f convolved with h.
Associative: h convolved with the result of f convolved with g equals the result of h convolved with f, then convolved with g.
Distributive: h convolved with the sum of f and g equals h convolved with f plus h convolved with g.
These properties show that convolution is algebraically structured, and they will connect directly to Fourier analysis.
You’re invited to try a quick quiz:
If y of n equals h of n convolved with x of n,
prove that y of n minus k equals h of n convolved with x of n minus k.
This follows naturally from the meaning of convolution.

Associative: h convolved with the result of f convolved with g equals the result of h convolved with f, then convolved with g.
Distributive: h convolved with the sum of f and g equals h convolved with f plus h convolved with g.
These properties show that convolution is algebraically structured, and they will connect directly to Fourier analysis.

slides.title: We can even prove these properties. I won’t go through the full proof now, but i
bullets: Here, we start with the standard convolution definition:
y of n equals the sum over k of x of k times h of n minus k.
By substituting k equals n minus capital K,
we change the index of summation and rewrite the expression in reversed order.
This leads directly to:
h convolved with x equals x convolved with h.
So, convolution is commutative — just like multiplication.

slides.script: We can actually prove that convolution is commutative, meaning the order of the input signal and the impulse response doesn't change the result. This follows directly from the definition of convolution, where we can simply swap the variables and rearrange the terms.

slides.title: A related concept to convolution is cross-correlation.
bullets: Mathematically, the key difference is in the sign of the shift.
In convolution, we compute the integral of f of x minus u times g of u.
In cross-correlation, it’s f of x plus u times g of u.
So, convolution flips one of the functions before shifting and multiplying,
while cross-correlation does not.
Both are useful in signal processing —
but for different purposes, like system response versus pattern matching.

spotlightEvents.label: Key difference in sign of shift
Cross-correlation computes integral with plus sign

slides.title: Let’s take a closer look at the graphical differences between convolution, cross
bullets: On the left, we see convolution.
Function f is in blue, and function g is in red.
In convolution, g is first flipped horizontally, then shifted, multiplied with f, and finally integrated or summed.
As g slides across f, the overlapping area is computed at each step — producing the black output curve below.
In the middle, we have cross-correlation.
It follows the same steps as convolution, except there’s no flipping of g.
We simply shift g across f, multiply at each point, and accumulate the result.
So visually, the shapes are the same, but the symmetry is different — this matters in pattern alignment and signal comparison.
On the right, we see autocorrelation.
This is a special case of cross-correlation where the same function is compared with itself.
So g and f are the same, and we measure how well the signal matches its own shifted versions.
The result usually peaks at zero shift — showing maximum similarity with itself — and falls off on either side.

Let’s take a closer look at the graphical differences between convolution, cross-correlation, and autocorrelation.
On the left, we see convolution.Function f is in blue, and function g is in red.In convolution, g is first flipped horizontally, then shifted, multiplied with f, and finally integrated or summed.As g slides across f, the overlapping area is computed at each step — producing the black output curve below.

slides.script: Let's take a closer look at the graphical differences between convolution, cross-correlation, and autocorrelation. Convolution involves flipping one function, shifting it, multiplying point by point, and integrating or summing. This process produces a symmetric output curve. In contrast, cross-correlation follows the same steps but without the flipping step, resulting in a non-symmetric output curve.

slides.title: Cross-correlation is deeply connected to the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality.
bullets: This fundamental result tells us that the inner product of two signals
is always less than or equal to the product of their norms.
Mathematically, this absolute value of the inner product is less than or equal to
the square root of the sum of all the a k squared
times the square root of the sum of all the b k squared.
Equality holds only when the two signals are proportional.
This is why cross-correlation works in this way:
the output is maximized when the signal and the template are aligned in shape —
a key idea in pattern recognition and signal detection.
slides.fullText:
Cross-correlation is deeply connected to the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality.
This fundamental result tells us that the inner product of two signalsis always less than or equal to the product of their norms.
Mathematically, this absolute value of the inner product is less than or equal tothe square root of the sum of all the a k squaredtimes the square root of the sum of all the b k squared.

Equality holds only when the two signals are proportional.
This is why cross-correlation works in this way:the output is maximized when the signal and the template are aligned in shape —a key idea in pattern recognition and signal detection.
slides.script: The Cauchy–Schwarz inequality is a fundamental concept that explains why cross-correlation works the way it does. It tells us that the inner product of two signals is always less than or equal to the product of their norms, with equality holding only when the two signals are proportional. This means that cross-correlation is maximized when the signal and the template are aligned in shape, which is why it's a powerful tool for detecting patterns and features.

slides.title: Here we observe a practical example of cross-correlation in action —
bullets: a technique known as matched filtering, widely used in signal detection.
In the top plot, the blue curve shows a received signal that includes both the desired pulse and a lot of background noise.
The red curve represents the matched filter — a reference copy of the signal we’re trying to detect.
The bottom plot shows the cross-correlation output.
Notice the sharp peak centered around one second — this is where the received signal best aligns with the matched filter.
That spike tells us that the target signal is present at that location in time.
This is exactly what the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality predicted:
the inner product, or correlation, reaches its maximum value when the two signals are best aligned.
Matched filtering like this is essential in many fields:
from radar and sonar, to wireless communication, and even in medical imaging —
anywhere we want to pull a weak signal out of noisy data.
Cross-correlation gives us a way to do that precisely and efficiently.

slides.title: Now let’s see a very practical use of convolution: feature extraction, especiall
bullets: On the left, we have the source image, shown as a grid of pixel intensity values.
We pick one pixel location, and we look at its local neighborhood, a 3-by-3 patch.
In the middle, we have a 3-by-3 convolution filter.
This one is the Sobel operator, often used to measure an intensity change in a specific direction.
You can see the weights include negative values on one side, positive values on the other,
with a stronger weight of two in the middle row.
That pattern is designed to respond strongly when pixel values change across the neighborhood.
Here is the procedure.
We align the Sobel kernel on top of a 3-by-3 region of the input image.
Then we multiply each pixel by the corresponding kernel weight,
and we add up all those products.
The slide even shows one full calculation.
When you do that sum of products for this highlighted patch, the result comes out to minus three.
And that number becomes the value of the destination pixel at that location on the right.
Now we slide the same filter to the next location and repeat.
Slide, multiply, sum.
Over and over, for every pixel position.
And the interpretation is simple.
If the region is mostly uniform, the positive and negative contributions cancel,
and the output stays small.
But if there is a sharp intensity transition, the cancellation fails,
and the output becomes large in magnitude.
That is edge detection in one sentence:
convolution turns local intensity changes into a strong numerical response,
so edges and boundaries become easier to detect and analyze.
And later in medical imaging, this kind of filtering connects directly to tasks like
highlighting boundaries, detecting structures, and extracting features for segmentation and diagnosis.

slides.title: Now let’s look at a classic edge-detection result.
bullets: On the left is the original grayscale image.
On the right is the output of the Canny edge detector,
which is one of the most widely used edge-detection methods.
The main goal here is simple:
we want to convert an image into a clean map of boundaries,
so object outlines and important structures stand out clearly.
Canny works in several stages.
First, it smooths the image a little to reduce noise,
because noise can create fake edges.
Next, it computes image gradients, which you can think of as measuring how quickly intensity changes.
In practice, this gradient step is done using convolution-based derivative filters,
so convolution is still at the core.
Then the algorithm looks for places where the gradient is locally strongest,
and it keeps only those locations.
That step is called non-maximum suppression, and it makes the edges thin and sharp.
Finally, Canny uses two thresholds, a high and a low,
and it links edge pixels together using a rule called hysteresis.
The idea is to keep strong edges for sure,
and keep weak edges only if they connect to strong ones.
That helps remove isolated noise responses while preserving real boundaries.
So what you get at the end is a crisp edge map, like the one shown on the right.
And this is exactly why edge detection matters:
it gives you a compact representation of structure,
which is often the first step toward higher-level tasks like segmentation, tracking, and measurement.
slides.fullText:
Now let’s look at a classic edge-detection result.

On the left is the original grayscale image.
On the right is the output of the Canny edge detector,
which is one of the most widely used edge-detection methods.

The main goal here is simple:
we want to convert an image into a clean map of boundaries,
so object outlines and important structures stand out clearly.

Canny works in several stages.

First, it smooths the image a little to reduce noise,
because noise can create fake edges.

Next, it computes image gradients, which you can think of as measuring how quickly intensity changes.
In practice, this gradient step is done using convolution-based derivative filters,
so convolution is still at the core.

Then the algorithm looks for places where the gradient is locally strongest,
and it keeps only those locations.
That step is called non-maximum suppression, and it makes the edges thin and sharp.

Finally, Canny uses two thresholds, a high and a low,
and it links edge pixels together using a rule called hysteresis.
The idea is to keep strong edges for sure,
and keep weak edges only if they connect to strong ones.
That helps remove isolated noise responses while preserving real boundaries.

So what you get at the end is a crisp edge map, like the one shown on the right.
And this is exactly why edge detection matters:
it gives you a compact representation of structure,
which is often the first step toward higher-level tasks like segmentation, tracking, and measurement.

The Canny edge detector works in several stages, starting with smoothing the image to reduce noise.

slides.title: Alright, let’s wrap up what we covered today.
bullets: The big storyline of this lecture is right at the top of the slide:
linear system, then shift invariance, and that leads directly to convolution.
First, we reviewed what it means for an operator to be linear.
It has to satisfy additivity and homogeneity,
which is just the superposition principle.
Next, we brought in shift invariance,
meaning the system behaves the same way even when the input is shifted in time or space.
And once you have both of those together, linear plus shift-invariant,
you get a very powerful result:
the system output can be written as a convolution of the input with the impulse response.
We also spent time building intuition for where convolution comes from,
by decomposing signals using impulses, shifting, scaling, and then summing everything up.
And along the way, we connected these ideas to practical examples,
like filtering, edge detection, and even imaging-system behavior.
So if convolution still feels a little unfamiliar, that’s normal.
The good news is that it becomes much more intuitive with repetition,
because it always follows the same recipe:
flip, shift, multiply, and add or integrate.
That’s the core tool we will keep using as we move forward in signal processing and medical imaging.

slides.title: Alright, now it’s time to apply what we’ve learned in a concrete way.
bullets: This is Homework 04.
For this assignment, assume the RC time constant is one second.
Your task is to use MATLAB to reproduce the plots shown in the first row on the right side of this slide.
Here is the setup.
The system is the RC circuit, and its impulse response is
h of t equals RC times e to the minus t over RC, multiplied by the unit step function.
With RC equal to one, that becomes a simple decaying exponential that starts at time zero.
You will define the input signal x of t shown in the first row,
compute the output y of t by convolving x of t with h of t,
and then plot the input and the output so they match the first-row figures.
As you do this, pay attention to the main idea:
the output is shaped by how the input overlaps with the impulse response over time.
That is exactly what convolution means.

In that document, include your MATLAB script and the figures you generated.
